one_line_takeaway: A frozen teacher LLM can be made resistant to logit-based knowledge distillation by learning a small low-rank matrix that post-processes output logits to minimize the conditional mutual information I(X;Z|Y), degrading cloned students (often below SFT) while preserving the teacher's own accuracy.
key_finding: The learned transformation consistently degrades distilled-student accuracy across teacher-student pairs, datasets, and four KD algorithms while the defended teacher's own accuracy stays close to the original. On GSM8K, distilling Qwen2.5-1.5B via AlphaNet drops from 62.93% to 50.95% (-11.98%). On MATH-500, KD of Qwen2.5-3B drops from 50.80% to 44.00% (-6.8%) while the teacher's own MATH accuracy only drops 53.8%->50.4%. In several cases distillation with defended logits falls below the SFT-only baseline (e.g. GSM8K Qwen2.5-1.5B AlphaNet 50.95 vs SFT-only 58.83), indicating the perturbed logits actively mislead rather than merely fail to help. The defense is stronger on GSM8K (long multi-step reasoning where perturbations accumulate) than on MMLU (single multiple-choice selections), and stronger on the white-box surrogate students (avg GSM8K drop 8.79%) than on black-box students (avg 4.52%). Loss-landscape analysis shows L_grad stabilizes near -0.2, i.e. transformed-vs-original student gradients diverge to ~101.54 degrees.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a defense that makes a proprietary (teacher) LLM resistant to logit-based knowledge distillation (KD), which the authors claim is the first defense targeting logit-based (as opposed to text/sequence-level) KD. The key idea is information-theoretic: they characterize the distillation-useful signal in teacher logits as the conditional mutual information I(X;Z|Y) between input queries X and teacher logits Z given the ground-truth next token Y, and argue that logit-based KD exploits exactly this 'contextual information beyond the label'. The defense learns a lightweight, low-rank transformation matrix M = E + AB (identity plus rank-r update, ~0.1% of model parameters) that post-processes the frozen teacher's output logits Z into Z' = M*Z to minimize I(X;Z|Y) while preserving task utility. They prove (Theorem 2) that any such matrix transformation forms a Markov chain Y->X->Z->Z' and can only reduce CMI, and (Theorem 3) that minimizing I(X;Z|Y) is exactly an Information Bottleneck objective with beta=1, i.e. I(X;Z|Y)=I(X;Z)-I(Z;Y). This decomposes into two sub-goals optimized by two losses: (1) L_CE (cross-entropy between Z' and true label) to maximize I(Z;Y) and preserve utility, justified by Lemma 4 that cross-entropy upper-bounds H(Y|Z); (2) L_grad to minimize I(X;Z), implemented by simulating KD against a surrogate student and maximizing the angular deviation (cosine dissimilarity) between the student gradients induced by original logits Z and transformed logits Z'.
threat_model: Defender is a commercial provider exposing a valuable proprietary LLM only as a black-box API. Adversary cannot access weights/architecture but CAN query the model and obtain output logits (full token probability distribution), then use them to run logit-based KD to cheaply clone the teacher's capabilities into a smaller student. A key stated constraint of the attack is that logit-based KD requires teacher and student to share the same tokenizer/vocabulary for one-to-one token correspondence (hence experiments use same-family model pairs). The defender's goal is to post-process emitted logits so that any student distilled from them is degraded (ideally below even label-only SFT), while the teacher's own answer accuracy and text fluency are preserved. The defense is preventive (blocks extraction) rather than post-hoc, distinguishing it from watermarking/fingerprinting which only enable after-the-fact ownership attribution.
limitations: Authors acknowledge (Appendix E) no rigorous proof that maximizing L_grad (gradient-angle deviation) is equivalent to maximizing I(X;Z); it is only a well-motivated heuristic surrogate validated empirically. The attack model is restrictive: logit-based KD requires a shared tokenizer, so all experiments use same-family teacher/student pairs (Qwen2.5, Llama 3) and the method is not shown against cross-family or purely text-based distillation. Absolute black-box degradations are often modest (a few points; e.g. GSM8K KD Qwen2.5-3B only 73.09->71.65) and the defense is weaker on MMLU. An adaptive adversary who does not use logits (text-only KD), uses a different surrogate, or applies logit denoising/calibration is not evaluated. The defense also assumes the provider is willing to return logits at all and slightly lowers teacher accuracy (a defense-vs-utility tradeoff tuned via lambda and rank r).

datasets_used: GSM8K (7,473 train / 1,319 eval, math word problems with reasoning chains)
MMLU (15,000 sampled train pairs with generated reasoning traces / 14,042 test examples, multiple-choice)
MATH (7,500 train with step-by-step solutions; evaluated on MATH-500)
quantitative_results: GSM8K Qwen2.5-1.5B via AlphaNet: 62.93% (Vanilla) -> 50.95% (Ours), a -11.98% drop
MATH-500 Qwen2.5-3B via KD: 50.80% (Vanilla) -> 44.00% (Ours), -6.8%; teacher MATH accuracy 53.8% -> 50.4%
Several defended-distillation results fall below SFT-only (e.g. GSM8K Qwen2.5-1.5B: Ours 50.95 vs SFT-only 58.83)
Average black-box student drop on GSM8K 4.52% vs white-box surrogate drop 8.79%; MATH-500 black-box 5.55% vs surrogate 6.55%
L_grad converges to ~ -0.2 (gradient angle ~101.54 degrees); matrix adds ~0.1% of total model parameters; default rank r=32, lambda=1
baselines_compared: Classical logit-based KD with forward KLD (Hinton et al.)
Reverse KLD / MiniLLM (RKLD, Gu et al.)
